content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 3,
1994
Honorable W.
E. McClellan
Councilmember, City of El Ca:jon
7675 University Avenue
La Mesa,
CA
91941
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94-239
Dear Mr. McClellan:
This is in response to your letter requesting follow-up
advice regarding your responsibilities as an El Cajon City
Councilmember under the conflict—of--interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
Please note that Commission advice is the application of law
to a specific set of facts provided by the requestor.
Thus, this
letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does
not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
OUESTION
May you participate in the decision regarding a mobilehome
rent control ordinance,
even though a number of residents in the
city’s mobilehoine parks are sources of income to you?
CONCLUSION
According to your facts, the rent control decisions will
foreseeably and materially affect your sources of income.
Moreover, the “public generally” exception would not apply to your
facts.
Thus,
you may not make, participate in making,
or in any
way attempt to use your official position to influence the rent
control decisions.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807
.
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-94-239
Page
2
FACTS
According to the facts in your first advice request
(Advice
Letter No.
A-93-400)
and those provided in your letter of July 22,
1994, the City of El Cajon is currently reviewing the issue of
mobilehome rents in the city.
The city may also consider a
mobilehome rent control ordinance.
You stated that a number of residents in the city’s
mobilehomne parks are sources of income to you of more than $250 in
the past twelve months.
These individuals have purchased either a
car or services from your business, McClellan Buick.
In conjunction with your first request for advice, you
provided the following facts:
There are twenty—six mobileholue
parks in El Cajon, with a total of approximately 2,000 mobilehoiue
households.
You have thirteen sources of income who reside in
seven different mobilehome parks.
One of your sources of income
owns a mobilehome park in El Cajon.
In your letter of July 22,
1994,
you provided these
additional facts:
The population in mobilehome parks in the city
is less than 10 percent of the population of the city.
Mobilehomes constitute less than 10 percent of the homeowners or
households in the jurisdiction.
Mobilehome parks comprise less
that 50 percent of the business in the jurisdiction.
There are
less than 5,000 persons
whorn will be affected by a mobilehome rent
control ordinance -
ANALYSIS
Conflicts of Interest,
Generally
As was stated in our prior letter,
any person that has made a
payment to you of $250 or more over the past
12 months is a
potentially disqualifying economic interest.
2
(Section 87103(c);
Section 82030.)
Consequently,
you may not participate in any
decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
3 and material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on these sources of income.
2
We also discussed possible conflicts of interest by virtue of
your business interest.
(Section 87103(a)
and (d).)
However,
your request for follow—up advice focuses on possible effects on
your sources of income,
and not your business.
Thus,
we have
limited our discussion to your sources of income.
The effect of a decision is considered reasonably foreseeable
if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No. A-94-239
Page
3
In our prior letter we concluded, based on your information,
that the decision in question would foreseeably affect your
sources of income.
4
Thus,
we advised that if the decisions will
have a material financial effect on jy source of income,
you
would be required to disqualify yourself.
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the financial effect of a decision is material, depending
on the specific circumstances of each decision.
According to your
letter requesting advice on October 15,
1994,
you stated that the
effect would also be material.
5
Consequently, absent an
exception,
you are prohibited from participating in the rent
control decisions.
“Public Generally” Exception
Even when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.)
You have asked about the application of amended
Regulation 18703, the “public generally” exception.
On September 7,
1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a governmental decision will fall
within the “public generally” exception.
New Regulation 18703
provides that the “public generally” exception applies where both
subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
Significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
-
Section 87103.5 provides an exception for retail customers of a
business entity engaged in retail sales of goods or services.
However, this exception only applies where the retail customers of
the business constitute a significant segment of the public
generally,
and the amount of income received by the business
entity from the customer is not distinguishable from the amount of
income received from its other retail customers.
We have enclosed
Regulation 18703.5 for your information.
As stated above, the Commission cannot be a finder of fact in
providing advice.
Thus,
we must leave this determination of
materiality up to you and your city attorney within the
appropriate provisions of Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.6.


Our File No. A-94-239
Page
4
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade,
or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
-
In your letter of July 22,
1994,
you stated that the segment
affected by the decision in question did not reach any of the
thresholds in Regulation 18703.
Consequently, the “public
generally” exception would not apply and you may not participate
in the rent control decisions.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
Sincerely,
Steven G Churchwell
ieral Counsel
W. Wallace
el,
Legal Division
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.
